data.title: GSI Technology (GSIT) Premium P/S And Ongoing Losses Test Bullish Narratives Ahead Of Q3 2026
data.description:
GSI Technology (GSIT) has just posted its Q3 2026 scorecard, with recent quarters showing revenue around US$6.4 million in Q2 2026 and a basic EPS loss of US$0.11, while the trailing twelve months reflect a basic EPS loss of US$0.43 and net income losses of US$11.66 million. Over the past six reported quarters, revenue has moved from US$4.55 million in Q2 2025 to US$6.44 million in Q2 2026, while basic EPS has ranged from a profit of US$0.04 in Q1 2025 to losses between US$0.08 and US$0.21. This sets up an earnings release where investors will be watching how much of that top line converts to less pressure on margins.

US$24.0m in sales, but TTM losses of US$11.7m

  • Over the trailing twelve months to Q2 2026, GSI generated US$24.0m in revenue and reported a net loss of US$11.7m, with basic EPS at a loss of US$0.43.
  • What stands out for a cautiously bullish view is that losses have been reduced by an average of 9.2% per year over the past five years. However, the latest trailing period still shows an US$11.7m loss, so the improvement trend and the ongoing lack of profitability sit side by side for investors to weigh.
    • Supporters can point to the 9.2% annual reduction in losses as evidence that the business is moving in the right direction, even with TTM EPS still at a loss of US$0.43.
    • On the other hand, critics can point out that five years of loss reduction has not yet produced positive net income, with TTM losses still in the double digit millions at US$11.7m.

Quarterly revenue up to US$6.4m while EPS stays in the red

  • Looking at the last six reported quarters, revenue moved from US$4.55m in Q2 2025 to US$6.44m in Q2 2026, while basic EPS over that stretch ranged from a profit of US$0.04 in Q1 2025 to losses between US$0.08 and US$0.21 per share.
  • What is notable for the bullish side is that higher recent revenue of US$6.3m to US$6.4m in 2026 still coincides with quarterly net losses between about US$2.2m and US$3.2m. Anyone arguing the top line alone will fix profitability has to reckon with the fact that more sales have not yet translated into positive EPS.
    • Bulls who focus on Q2 2026 revenue of US$6.44m versus Q2 2025 revenue of US$4.55m need to explain why net income is still a loss of US$3.19m in the latest quarter.
    • At the same time, the spread in EPS outcomes, from a profit of US$0.04 in Q1 2025 to losses as wide as US$0.21 per share, shows that earnings have been quite variable around a generally loss making pattern.

10.6x P/S and recent dilution set a high bar

  • The shares trade on a P/S of 10.6x compared with the US Semiconductor industry at 5.8x and peers at 5.6x, while shareholders have also seen substantial dilution over the past year and the share price, at US$7.22, has been volatile over the last three months.
  • Bears argue that paying a P/S almost double industry and peer averages, at 10.6x, for a company with trailing twelve month losses of US$11.7m and recent dilution exposes investors to valuation and capital structure risks that are not yet offset by reported profitability.
    • The combination of substantial new shares issued and an elevated P/S multiple means each existing share represents a smaller claim on a business that is still loss making on US$24.0m of trailing revenue.
    • Recent share price volatility around US$7.22 also fits with that bearish argument that the market is still debating how to price an unprofitable company at a premium multiple.

data.title: LPL Financial (LPLA) Margin Compression Challenges Bullish Growth Narrative After FY 2025 Results
data.description:
LPL Financial Holdings (LPLA) just wrapped up FY 2025 with Q4 revenue of US$4.8 billion and basic EPS of US$3.76, alongside net income of US$300.7 million. This puts fresh numbers on the table for investors tracking its full year. The company has seen quarterly revenue move from US$3.4 billion in Q4 2024 to US$4.8 billion in Q4 2025, with basic EPS shifting from US$3.62 to US$3.76 over the same stretch, while trailing 12 month revenue sits at US$16.6 billion and EPS at US$10.97. Margins are firmly in focus here, as investors weigh these headline results against the pressure seen in profitability over the past year.

See our full analysis for LPL Financial Holdings.

With the numbers on the board, the next step is to see how this earnings print lines up with the prevailing stories about LPL and where the margin and growth narrative might need a rethink.

Curious how numbers become stories that shape markets? Explore Community Narratives

Advertisement

5.2% Net Margin Versus 8.8% Last Year

  • Over the last 12 months LPL converted US$16.6b of revenue into US$863.0 million of net income, which works out to a 5.2% net profit margin compared with 8.8% in the prior year.
  • What stands out for the bullish view that focuses on high earnings quality and 17.1% EPS compound growth is that this margin compression sits alongside solid reported profits, which creates a mixed picture:
    • Bulls point to trailing EPS of US$10.97 and Q4 FY 2025 EPS of US$3.76 as evidence that the business is still earning well per share, even with a lower margin.
    • At the same time, the drop from an 8.8% to a 5.2% net margin means more of that US$16.6b revenue is being absorbed by costs, so anyone leaning bullish has to be comfortable with this pressure on profitability.
In light of margins compressing while EPS remains solid, some investors are asking whether this is a blip or the start of a new normal for LPL's profitability story. 📊 Read the full LPL Financial Holdings Consensus Narrative.

Revenue Forecast Around 11% With Faster 22.1% Earnings Growth

  • Revenue is forecast to grow about 11% per year while earnings are expected to grow around 22.1% per year, which is faster than the 10.6% forecast for the wider US market in the data provided.
  • Consensus narrative thinking that sees LPL as a growth name finds some support here, but the recent margin profile adds a twist:
    • The combination of US$16.6b in trailing revenue and a 5.2% net margin implies that a lot of the expected 22.1% earnings growth would need to come from either stronger margins or continued top line expansion.
    • Because the latest trailing margin is below last year’s 8.8%, anyone leaning on the bullish growth story has to weigh whether forecasts are assuming a shift back toward that higher margin level or simply more revenue on today’s thinner margin base.

Valuation Tension: 33.8x P/E And Upside To Targets

  • At a P/E of 33.8x versus peer and industry averages of 19.9x and 23.9x, the shares at US$364.50 trade on a richer multiple. This is occurring even though the stock is about 6.7% below a fair value estimate and below the US$390.73 DCF fair value, while the analyst price target in the data sits at US$448.92.
  • For a bearish narrative that worries LPL is priced too highly, the numbers give both supporting evidence and some pushback:
    • The higher 33.8x P/E compared with peers, together with a trailing 5.2% net margin, lines up with the concern that investors are paying more for each dollar of earnings while profitability is not at last year’s 8.8% level.
    • On the other hand, the current US$364.50 share price sitting below the US$390.73 DCF fair value and under the US$448.92 analyst target means valuation models in the data are not unanimously flagging the stock as stretched at today’s level.

Margins soften with 2.4% net profit

data.title: U.S. Bancorp Leadership Shift Puts Focus On Execution And Technology Priorities
data.description:
• U.S. Bancorp (NYSE:USB) has appointed current CEO and President Gunjan Kedia as Chairman, succeeding Andy Cecere.
  • Long time executive Andy Cecere is retiring from his role as Chairman.
  • Chief Operating Officer Souheil Badran plans to retire this spring, with a succession process already underway.

For investors watching U.S. Bancorp, these leadership changes come as the stock trades at $56.33, with a 1 year return of 22.5% and a 5 year return of 50.7%. The company has also recorded a 3 year return of 32.2%, with shorter term moves of 0.3% over the past week and 3.7% over the past month.

As Gunjan Kedia takes on the combined role of Chairman and CEO, and with a COO transition ahead, you may want to monitor how the board describes any shifts in priorities or execution. Leadership stability, capital allocation choices, and any commentary on growth areas or risk controls could all influence how NYSE:USB trades during this period.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for U.S. Bancorp by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on U.S. Bancorp.

How U.S. Bancorp stacks up against its biggest competitors

The leadership transition at U.S. Bancorp places more authority with Gunjan Kedia, who will hold the combined Chairman, CEO and President roles, while COO Souheil Badran’s planned retirement creates an opening in the operations engine that has been focused on automation and AI-powered efficiency. For you as an investor, the key question is whether the board and Kedia can keep operational execution on track through this handover, especially as the bank balances growth, technology spending and capital return alongside large-bank peers like JPMorgan Chase, Bank of America and Wells Fargo.

Advertisement

How this fits the U.S. Bancorp Narrative

This leadership news ties directly into the existing narrative that highlights digital payments, tech investments and fee income as important drivers for U.S. Bancorp. Badran has been closely linked to automation and contact-center efficiency, so the succession plan will matter for how consistently the bank follows through on those efforts. Kedia’s expanded role could also shape how management prioritizes digital banking, capital markets expansion and long-term capital returns.

Risks and rewards investors should keep in mind

  • Continuity from an internal Chairman and CEO can support consistent messaging and follow through on previously outlined growth and efficiency priorities.
  • A planned, assisted COO transition with an existing succession program may help preserve progress on automation, AI and client-experience projects.
  • Concentrating power in one leader and replacing a key operations executive can increase execution risk if future decisions on cost control or technology spend misfire.
  • Any perceived disruption in operational performance could affect how investors compare U.S. Bancorp with larger, more diversified competitors.

What to watch next

From here, it is worth watching how the board and Kedia describe priorities for operations leadership, technology spending and capital allocation on upcoming calls, as well as any evidence that customer-service metrics or expense trends shift during the transition. If you want to see how these leadership moves fit into the longer-term story and how other investors are thinking about it, you can check community narratives on U.S. Bancorp’s dedicated page.

data.title: Tokyo Electron (TSE:8035) Might Become A Compounding Machine
data.description:
There are a few key trends to look for if we want to identify the next multi-bagger. In a perfect world, we'd like to see a company investing more capital into its business and ideally the returns earned from that capital are also increasing. Put simply, these types of businesses are compounding machines, meaning they are continually reinvesting their earnings at ever-higher rates of return. Return On Capital Employed (ROCE): What Is It?

Just to clarify if you're unsure, ROCE is a metric for evaluating how much pre-tax income (in percentage terms) a company earns on the capital invested in its business. To calculate this metric for Tokyo Electron, this is the formula:

Return on Capital Employed = Earnings Before Interest and Tax (EBIT) ÷ (Total Assets - Current Liabilities)

0.33 = JP¥687b ÷ (JP¥2.7t - JP¥560b) (Based on the trailing twelve months to September 2025).

So, Tokyo Electron has an ROCE of 33%. In absolute terms that's a great return and it's even better than the Semiconductor industry average of 11%.

We'd be pretty happy with returns on capital like Tokyo Electron. data.title: Lawsuit Over Loan Steering Puts Rocket Companies Business Model In Focus
data.description:
• A consumer class action lawsuit has been filed against Rocket Companies (NYSE:RKT), alleging illegal steering of homebuyers to Rocket Mortgage.
  • The suit claims borrowers were directed toward Rocket's mortgage products under disadvantageous loan terms.
  • Plaintiffs argue the conduct violated fair lending standards and harmed consumers seeking home financing.

Rocket Companies, the parent of Rocket Mortgage, focuses on digital mortgage origination and related financial services. For investors watching the housing and mortgage sector, legal challenges around how loans are offered and priced can affect how regulators, partners, and customers view a brand that relies heavily on trust and online distribution.

For you as an investor or prospective investor, this lawsuit is worth tracking because it adds a legal and regulatory angle to the existing business story for NYSE:RKT. The allegations touch on core questions of how Rocket connects homebuyers to mortgage products and how that might influence client behavior and future oversight.

data.title: National Fuel Gas (NFG) EPS Rebound Reinforces Bullish Valuation Narrative
data.description:
National Fuel Gas (NFG) opened fiscal Q1 2026 with total revenue of US$651.5 million and basic EPS of US$1.99, alongside net income excluding extra items of US$181.6 million. The company has seen quarterly revenue move from US$549.5 million and EPS of US$0.50 in Q1 2025 to US$651.5 million and EPS of US$1.99 in Q1 2026. Trailing twelve month EPS sits at US$7.23 on revenue of about US$2.4 billion, placing margins and earnings quality in focus for investors assessing the latest results.

See our full analysis for National Fuel Gas.

With the headline numbers on the table, the next step is to see how this earnings print lines up against the widely held narratives around National Fuel Gas's growth profile, risk factors, and income appeal.

Curious how numbers become stories that shape markets? Explore Community Narratives

Advertisement

US$2.4b trailing revenue sets the backdrop

  • Over the last twelve months, National Fuel Gas generated about US$2.4b in revenue and US$655.2 million in net income excluding extra items, with trailing EPS of US$7.23 giving you a fuller picture than any single quarter.
  • What stands out in the general market view is the mix of stability and cyclicality, and the trailing figures help you see that balance in practice:
    • Compared with Q1 2025, where net income excluding extra items was US$45.0 million, the latest Q1 print of US$181.6 million sits within a much stronger trailing earnings base of US$655.2 million.
    • The trailing EPS path from US$0.84 in Q4 2024 to US$7.23 by Q1 2026 highlights that the recent recovery is spread across several quarters, not just one strong period.
To see how these numbers fit into a bigger story investors are debating, check out the full narrative that has been built around them: 📊 Read the full National Fuel Gas Consensus Narrative.

P/E of 12.2x versus peers at 14.7x and 19x

  • The trailing P/E of 12.2x sits below the Global Gas Utilities industry average of 14.7x and a peer average of 19x, while the shares are also cited as around 33.1% below a stated fair value estimate.
  • Bulls argue that this kind of discount is hard to ignore, and the recent profit record is part of their case:
    • Earnings are recorded as having grown about 6.3% per year over the last five years, with the company now profitable on US$655.2 million of trailing net income excluding extra items. This aligns with the view that investors are not paying a high multiple for that earnings base.
    • At the same time, revenue is forecast to grow around 11.4% per year and earnings about 9% per year. From a bullish perspective, a 12.2x P/E and a share price of US$84 are seen as not fully reflecting those growth figures, especially when the DCF fair value is cited at US$125.54.

Debt load and dividend coverage in focus

  • The stock offers a 2.55% dividend yield, but that dividend is flagged as not well covered by free cash flow and the company is described as carrying a high level of debt.
  • Skeptics highlight these balance sheet and cash flow points as key counterweights to the more optimistic story:
    • Even with US$655.2 million in trailing net income excluding extra items, the fact that free cash flow coverage of the dividend is weak means income investors may want to look closely at how comfortably that 2.55% yield is supported.
    • The mention of a high level of debt, alongside ongoing investment needs across utility, pipeline, and upstream operations, is cited by bears as a reason to question how much flexibility the company has if conditions become less favorable.

Next Steps

Don't just look at this quarter; the real story is in the long-term trend. We've done an in-depth analysis on National Fuel Gas's growth and its valuation to see if today's price is a bargain. Add the company to your watchlist or portfolio now so you don't miss the next big move.
